title: Security Team Weekly Sync
transcript:
Dana Whitfield: Alright, it's ten o'clock, let's get rolling. Omar, Jess, Sam — everyone hear me okay?

Omar Haddad: Loud and clear.

Sam Rivera: I'm here. My camera's doing that frozen thing again but I'm here.

Jess Park: Morning. Give me one second, I'm closing about forty tabs.

Dana Whitfield: Take your time. Standard agenda today: phishing simulation results, MFA rollout status, vendor pipeline, and then one new item at the end. Jess, you want to kick off with the phishing numbers?

Jess Park: Sure. Let me share my screen. Can you see the dashboard?

Omar Haddad: We can see your inbox, actually.

Jess Park: Oh no. Okay, hang on. Wrong window. How about now?

Dana Whitfield: There we go. Dashboard's up.

Jess Park: Great. So the Q2 phishing simulation wrapped up last Thursday. Headline number: the click rate came in at 12%, down from 18% in Q1.

Dana Whitfield: Twelve. That's real movement. Nice work on the awareness push.

Omar Haddad: What was the lure this quarter?

Jess Park: A fake benefits-enrollment reminder. Timely, because HR had just sent the real pre-enrollment teaser in May, so it blended in.

Sam Rivera: That's genuinely mean. I respect it.

Jess Park: The interesting part is the distribution, though. Most departments were single digits. Claims and underwriting dragged the average up, and here's the kicker — about a third of the clicks came from people who also clicked in Q1.

Dana Whitfield: Repeat clickers. How many?

Jess Park: Forty-one people clicked in both quarters.

Omar Haddad: So what do we do — push the whole company through the training module again? People grumbled hard last time. I got three tickets that were basically complaints with a subject line.

Dana Whitfield: Yeah, I don't want to burn goodwill on the eighty-eight percent who didn't click. Here's my proposal: targeted retraining only for the repeat clickers. Not another all-staff round.

Jess Park: Agreed. Blanket training has diminishing returns anyway — the research on this is pretty consistent.

Omar Haddad: Fine by me. Less noise in my ticket queue.

Dana Whitfield: Okay, decision made: targeted retraining for repeat clickers only, not the whole company. Jess, coordinate with HR on how it gets assigned so it doesn't feel punitive.

Sam Rivera: Do the repeat clickers know who they are?

Jess Park: They will. Gently. It's framed as a refresher, not a detention.

Dana Whitfield: Good. Next up — MFA. Omar, where are we?

Omar Haddad: As of this morning we're at 64% of staff enrolled. Roughly 512 people out of the 800, give or take some contractors I'm still classifying.

Dana Whitfield: And the remaining thirty-six percent — what's the holdup?

Omar Haddad: A mix. Field adjusters who barely open their laptops, a batch of shared-mailbox accounts we have to untangle before we can enroll them properly, and the usual parade of 'I got a new phone' resets eating my techs' mornings.

Dana Whitfield: What's the finish line look like?

Omar Haddad: Still targeting end of June for completion. The long tail is always the fight, but end of June is the target.

Jess Park: Sorry, you cut out for a second there, Omar — did you say end of June?

Omar Haddad: End of June, yes. Confirmed.

Dana Whitfield: Okay, hold that date, I'm writing it down. Vendor pipeline next. Procurement flagged a new one — CloudCRM. The sales team wants it for managing the policy-renewal pipeline.

Jess Park: What data would actually go into it?

Dana Whitfield: Customer contact data at minimum. Possibly policy details, depending on how sales configures it. Which is exactly why I want a proper vendor risk review before this thing builds momentum.

Omar Haddad: It always builds momentum. By the time we see these, someone's already had the demo and fallen in love.

Dana Whitfield: Which is why we're getting ahead of it. Jess, can you run the vendor risk review? Taylor Brooks is the procurement lead on it — set up a session with Taylor and go through it together.

Jess Park: On it. I'll send CloudCRM our security questionnaire today and request their SOC 2 report.

Omar Haddad: Get their SSO story too. I've been burned by CRM vendors where single sign-on turns out to be a premium add-on.

Jess Park: Adding it to the list.

Dana Whitfield: Last item, and it's a new one. The executive team asked me last week what AI tools our staff are actually using. And I had to say, honestly, I don't fully know. Which is not a sentence I enjoy saying to executives.

Sam Rivera: I can take that.

Dana Whitfield: I was hoping you'd say that. Sam, I want a shadow-AI inventory — which unapproved AI tools staff are using day to day, and roughly what kind of data might be going into them. Can you have it in two weeks?

Sam Rivera: Two weeks works. How do I actually find them, though? Just survey people?

Omar Haddad: I can pull DNS logs and the web filter's AI category hits for you. That'll catch most of the browser-based stuff.

Sam Rivera: Perfect. DNS logs plus maybe a short anonymous survey, then, so people admit the ones the filter misses.

Dana Whitfield: Anonymous is the right call. We want honesty, not hiding.

Jess Park: And no naming and shaming in the report, please. I watched that go badly at my last company — usage went underground within a month.

Sam Rivera: Noted. No names, just tools and data types. So that's due June 16?

Dana Whitfield: Two weeks, June 16, yes. Okay, quick recap before we break: targeted retraining for the forty-one repeat clickers, Omar drives MFA to done by end of June, Jess runs the CloudCRM vendor risk review with Taylor Brooks, and Sam delivers the shadow-AI inventory in two weeks.

Omar Haddad: And I'll send managers the MFA laggard list this week so it's not just me nagging.

Dana Whitfield: Perfect. That's everything — I'm giving you eleven minutes back. Go spend them wisely.
